message:
fix(core): Fix test ownership and clippy warnings in lock.rs

- Fix use-after-move in acquire_and_release_lock test by saving meta_path before drop
- Add #[must_use] attributes to public accessor methods
- Replace file.unlock() with drop() idiom
- Add clippy allow for option_if_let_else

Also includes policy.rs additions for PolicyGatedInjector (wa-4vx.8.5):
- Add InjectionResult enum for policy-gated action outcomes
- Add PolicyGatedInjector for coordinating policy + action execution
- Add comprehensive tests for injection result handling

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-4vx.6.4","title":"Daemon integration tests using synthetic deltas (no WezTerm required)","description":"# Task: Daemon integration tests (synthetic deltas)

## Goal
Test the full observation pipeline without requiring a live WezTerm instance.

This is the test bed for the core architecture:
`ingest → persist segments → pattern engine → persist detections/events`.

## Approach
- Construct a synthetic pane + domain model.
- Feed synthetic `PaneDelta` segments into the event bus:
  - include prompt markers / OSC 133 boundaries
  - include text that should trigger known patterns
  - include a deliberate discontinuity to force a GAP path

- Assert end-to-end outcomes:
  - segments are persisted
  - gaps are recorded when expected
  - pattern engine emits detections
  - detections are persisted as events

## Determinism requirements
- No timing-based assertions.
- Use deterministic clocks or explicit timestamps in test inputs.
- Use temp DBs and fixed ordering.

## Logging requirements
- Tests should capture structured logs and assert:
  - ingest tick ran
  - counts of segments/events written
  - no panics or busy loops

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests pass in CI environments without WezTerm.
- A regression in any stage (ingest/persist/pattern/event) fails with a clear assertion.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Include at least one fixture that would *not* match any pattern to ensure quick-reject/no-match path is exercised.
  - Include an explicit “GAP required” fixture and assert the GAP record exists (prove the discontinuity path is truly tested).

- Observability assertions:
  - Assert structured log fields include per-pane identifiers and stage counters so failures can be triaged quickly.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.6.5","title":"Structured logging baseline: tracing spans, per-pane correlation ids, log-to-file option","description":"# Task: Structured logging baseline

## Goal
Make `wa` debuggable under real load and make E2E/diagnostic artifacts useful.

This is the baseline logging layer used by:
- `wa watch` runtime
- robot/human CLI commands
- E2E harness artifacts (`wa-4vx.10.6`, `wa-4vx.10.11`)

## Requirements
- Use `tracing` + `tracing-subscriber` (no ad-hoc `println!`).
- Support both:
  - human-friendly pretty logs (interactive default)
  - JSON logs (machine-parseable; required for CI/E2E/ops)

## Required fields / correlation
Key spans and events must include (where applicable):
- `workspace`
- `domain`
- `pane_id`
- `window_id`/`tab_id` (when available)
- `rule_id` / `event_id`
- `workflow_name` / `execution_id`
- `action_id` (audit linkage)

## Configuration
- `log_level`:
  - from config
  - overridable via `RUST_LOG`
- `log_format`: `pretty | json`
- `log_file`: optional path; when set, logs are written to file (E2E/diag bundles rely on this)

## Safety / redaction
- Never log raw pane contents.
- Any user-provided text that could contain secrets must be logged only via the redaction layer (`wa-4vx.8.3`).
- JSON logs must not include secrets, device codes, auth tokens, cookies, or full DOM dumps.

## Implementation notes
- Prefer a single global subscriber configured once at startup.
- Ensure JSON logging is valid even under concurrent spans.
- Include a stable timestamp field for JSON logs.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - logger initialization does not panic
  - JSON log lines parse as JSON
  - required correlation fields appear on key spans (where practical via helper macros)
  - redaction invariants: known secret patterns do not appear in emitted log lines

- E2E:
  - harness captures logs reliably and includes them in artifacts (`wa-4vx.10.6`, `wa-4vx.10.11`)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Running `wa watch` emits structured logs with pane/domain correlation fields.
- JSON logging mode produces parseable JSON lines.
- E2E runs can enable verbose logging and capture it to an artifacts directory without code changes.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.6.6","title":"Watcher IPC receiver: accept user-var events over local socket; update state; emit signals","description":"# Task: Watcher IPC receiver (user-var events)

## Goal
Allow short-lived `wa event` processes (spawned by WezTerm) to deliver pane signals to the long-running watcher.

## Design
- Watcher opens a local unix socket (path in config).
- Message format: small JSON envelope:
  - pane_id
  - event_type (e.g., user_var, status_update)
  - name
  - value (raw + optional decoded JSON)
  - received_at
- Receiver task:
  - accepts connections
  - reads one message
  - validates size limits
  - enqueues into the event bus as a `Signal` event

## How the watcher uses the signal
- Update per-pane state if relevant (e.g., prompt boundary checkpoint or status_update metadata).
- Optionally persist as a separate table (`pane_signals`) if we want historical record.

## Failure behavior
- If message is invalid/too large: reject with an error (do not crash watcher).
- Logging: do not log raw payload content by default.

## Testing
- Unit/integration test starts the receiver, sends a synthetic event, and observes it on the event bus.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A synthetic `wa event` message reaches the watcher and appears on the internal event bus.
- Invalid payloads are rejected safely with clear errors and without logging raw secrets.

-{"id":"wa-4vx.6.7","title":"Watcher single-instance lock: prevent multiple wa watch writers per workspace","description":"# Task: Watcher single-instance lock (workspace guard)

## Goal
Prevent accidental multi-instance runs of `wa watch` that:
- compete for the same SQLite DB and cause busy loops or confusing interleavings
- duplicate ingest work and distort event timelines
- race on IPC endpoints (user-var lane socket)

Users should get a clear, actionable error instead of subtle corruption or "it feels flaky".

## Behavior (v0)
- On startup, `wa watch` acquires an exclusive lock scoped to the selected workspace/DB.
- Lock file lives under the workspace scope (`wa-4vx.9.6` conventions).
- If the lock is already held:
  - exit non-zero with a stable error code
  - print a message that includes:
    - workspace path
    - pid/host if recorded
    - a safe remediation (stop the other watcher, or run with an explicit override)

## Locking details
- Use an OS-level file lock (not just a pid file).
- Record diagnostic metadata adjacent to the lock (pid, started_at, wa version) for humans.

## Overrides (escape hatch)
Provide an explicit, scary flag (proposed): `--dangerous-disable-lock`.

Rules:
- If `--dangerous-disable-lock` is used, require the user to also provide an explicit `--db <path>` that is distinct, OR print a warning that they may corrupt timelines.

The goal is to make “I really want two watchers” possible, but never accidental.

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests:
  - first instance acquires lock successfully
  - second instance fails with stable error + hint
  - lock is released on clean shutdown
  - crash-y path: simulate a process exit and confirm the lock is not permanently wedged

## Acceptance Criteria
- Running two `wa watch` processes against the same workspace fails fast and explains why.
- The lock mechanism is robust across crashes (lock releases when process dies).

 {"id":"wa-4vx.6.8","title":"Watcher lifecycle hardening: SIGINT/SIGTERM shutdown + panic crash report","description":"# Task: Watcher lifecycle hardening

## Goal
Make `wa watch` behave like a trustworthy daemon:
- shuts down cleanly on SIGINT/SIGTERM
- flushes and closes storage without leaving the DB in a confusing partial state
- produces actionable crash artifacts on panic

This is pure UX/reliability: users must be able to stop wa without fear.

## Requirements
### Graceful shutdown
- On SIGINT/SIGTERM:
  - stop ingest polling
  - drain/close channels
  - flush StorageHandle writer queue
  - close IPC endpoints
  - release workspace lock
- Emit a short shutdown summary (logs):
  - elapsed
  - final queue depths
  - last persisted seq per pane (optional if cheap)

### Panic/crash reporting
- Install a panic hook that:
  - writes a crash report artifact in the workspace artifacts dir
  - includes backtrace + build/version + workspace id
  - includes last-known health snapshot (queue depth, ingest lag)
  - is redacted (no raw pane text)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - shutdown path drains queue and closes cleanly
- Integration tests:
  - synthetic ingest load + SIGINT simulation → clean exit
  - panic simulation → crash report file layout + redaction
- E2E:
  - `wa-4vx.10.15` graceful shutdown
  - `wa-4vx.10.21` `wa stop` coordination

## Acceptance Criteria
- Ctrl-C during heavy ingest does not lose already-enqueued writes.
- A panic produces an artifact that enables debugging without reproducing.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.7","title":"[EPIC] Robot mode CLI (stable JSON envelope + core commands)","description":"# Robot mode CLI

## Goal
Provide an agent-first CLI surface that is:
- stable (schema + error codes)
- token-efficient
- composable
- functionally complete for core operations

Robot mode is how an AI agent controls wa without relying on UI.

## Contract
Every robot command returns a consistent JSON envelope:
- ok/data/error/hint/elapsed_ms/version/now

## Core commands (minimum)
- `wa robot state` — list panes + inferred agent/state
- `wa robot get-text <pane_id>` — read tail or full text
- `wa robot send <pane_id> <text>` — send input (guarded)
- `wa robot wait-for <pane_id> <pattern>` — wait with timeout
- `wa robot search <fts query>` — query SQLite FTS
- `wa robot events` — recent/unhandled events
- `wa robot workflow <name> <pane_id>` — trigger workflow (Phase 2 will implement)
- `wa robot quick-start` — high-density help output

## Why this exists
Human-first CLIs are too verbose and inconsistent for agent consumption.
Robot mode must be deterministic so agents can compose reliable loops.

## Acceptance
- All commands work without interactive prompts.
- Error codes are stable and actionable.



## Success Criteria
- `wa robot` commands have stable JSON envelopes and versioned schemas for outputs/errors.
- Robot surfaces can query panes/state/events/search and perform policy-gated actions (send/workflow) safely.
- All robot outputs are validated in unit tests against JSON schemas.
- E2E scripts exercise robot commands as part of end-to-end scenarios with detailed artifacts.


## Testing
- Schema validation:
  - Robot outputs and errors are validated against versioned JSON schemas (see `wa-4vx.7.10`).
  - Tests must validate stable `error.code` values and stable envelope shape across commands.

- Contract tests:
  - For each command, include “happy path” + at least one failure mode (pane missing, invalid args, DB not initialized, policy denied).
  - Ensure error messages remain actionable but do not leak secrets.

- E2E coverage:
  - Robot commands are exercised as building blocks inside end-to-end scenarios (search, send gating, workflow triggers) with artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.7.1","title":"Robot mode scaffolding: CLI subcommands + stable JSON envelope + error codes","description":"# Task: Robot mode scaffolding

## Goal
Build the foundational CLI plumbing for `wa robot`.

Robot mode is an API surface for other tools/agents. It must be:
- stable
- machine-parseable
- explicit about errors and hints

## Requirements
### Subcommand structure
- Define a clear subcommand tree per plan.
- Centralize handler dispatch.

### Global flags (critical UX)
All robot subcommands must accept consistent global flags:
- --workspace <path> / WA_WORKSPACE
- --config <path> (optional)

Robots must not guess where the DB is.

### Output contract
- Stable JSON envelope:
  - ok / data / error / hint / elapsed_ms / version / now
- Stable error codes (string constants).

## Deliverables
- CLI parsing + dispatch to handlers.
- Shared response builder.
- Shared "context" builder that resolves:
  - effective config
  - workspace paths (DB/lock/socket/log)
  - and hands them to handlers

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests:
  - envelope shape is stable and schema-validated (`wa-4vx.7.10`)
  - unknown subcommand yields a structured error
  - `--workspace` changes scope deterministically
  - handlers can be tested with a fixture workspace without a real WezTerm

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa robot help` prints stable JSON.
- Unknown subcommand yields a structured error.
- Passing --workspace changes the DB/workspace scope deterministically.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.8.2","title":"Rate limiting: per-pane, per-action-kind (avoid spam / runaway loops)","description":"# Task: Rate limiting

## Goal
Prevent runaway automation by enforcing rate limits such as:
- cap sends per pane per minute
- cap high-risk actions per interval (e.g., browser auth, approvals)

This is a safety primitive: even correct logic can become harmful if it loops.

## Design choices
- Prefer a deterministic, testable algorithm:
  - token bucket (recommended) or fixed-window counters
- Keying should be explicit:
  - per `(pane_id, action_kind)`
  - optionally incorporate `actor_kind` (robot vs human vs workflow) if needed

## Behavior
- On each action, the PolicyEngine consults the limiter.
- When over limit, return either:
  - `RequireApproval` (preferred for recoverability), OR
  - `Deny` for truly dangerous actions

The decision should include:
- the specific limit that triggered
- the retry-after duration (if meaningful)
- remediation hints (wait, reduce concurrency, change config)

## Configuration
- Limits are configurable via wa config with clear defaults.
- Include sensible “safe defaults” so new users don’t get spammed.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - limits trigger deterministically
  - counters reset/roll over correctly
  - separate panes do not share budgets
  - separate action kinds do not share budgets
  - `retry_after` is sensible and monotonic

## Acceptance Criteria
- Exceeding a configured limit yields `RequireApproval` or `Deny` with a clear reason.
- Rate limiting behavior is deterministic and test-covered (no timing flakiness).

 {"id":"wa-4vx.8.3","title":"Secret redaction for audit logs (tokens, api keys, passwords) + safe logging conventions","description":"# Task: Secret redaction

## Goal
Ensure no secrets leak into logs, robot mode output, or diagnostic bundles.

## Deliverables
- `Redactor` with a conservative set of regex patterns:
  - common token/key formats (OpenAI-style `sk-...`, GitHub `ghp_...`, etc.)
  - generic `token=...`, `password: ...`, etc.
- Logging conventions:
  - never log raw device codes
  - never log OAuth URLs with embedded params

## Testing
- Unit tests confirm known secret patterns are replaced with `[REDACTED]`.
- Tests include both:
  - true positives (must redact)
  - false positives to avoid (do not over-redact benign strings)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Known secret patterns are redacted across audit/log/export paths.
- Redaction is conservative and does not require callers to remember to “do the right thing” manually.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.8.4","title":"Policy rules loaded from config (allow/deny/require approval) + robot-safe errors","description":"# Task: Policy rules from config

## Goal
Allow operators to tune safety without recompiling:
- allowlist safe actions
- deny known-unsafe actions
- require approval for high-risk patterns

This is a major UX + safety dial:
- lets cautious users run in “require approval” mode initially
- lets advanced users allowlist known-safe panes/actions

## Deliverables
- Config schema for policy rules (via `wa.toml` schema work).
- Rule evaluation order and precedence (must be documented and deterministic).
- In robot/mcp mode, `RequireApproval` must surface as a structured outcome including allow-once metadata.

## Rule model (suggested)
- Match on (at least):
  - action kind (send_text, ctrl_c, workflow_step, mcp_tool)
  - pane_id/domain/title/cwd (where applicable)
  - inferred agent type (optional)
- Decisions:
  - allow
  - deny
  - require_approval

## Precedence
- Hard denies (capability gates, e.g., alt-screen) always win.
- Explicit per-workspace rules override defaults.
- Specific match beats general match (document tie-break rules).

## Testing
- Unit tests (see `wa-4vx.8.6`):
  - precedence rules are deterministic
  - require-approval emits stable allow-once payload
  - deny cannot be overridden by approval
- Integration/E2E:
  - `wa-4vx.10.16` uses a config rule to force `RequireApproval` and validates full approval loop

## Acceptance Criteria
- A sample config rule changes behavior deterministically.
- Robot/human surfaces expose `RequireApproval` with actionable remediation (how to approve).

-{"id":"wa-4vx.8.5","title":"Integrate PolicyEngine into all SendText paths (robot send + workflows)","description":"# Task: Integrate PolicyEngine into input injection paths (SendText / Ctrl-C)

## Goal
Guarantee that **no code path** can inject terminal input without passing policy checks.

## Integration points
- `wa robot send`
- `wa send` (human)
- workflow engine step actions
- any helper utilities that send Ctrl-C / control sequences

## Architecture (avoid duplication)
Implement a single shared helper in core (proposed name):
- `PolicyGatedInjector`

Responsibilities:
1. Build a `PolicyInput` (actor kind, pane id, action kind, redacted summary).
2. Call `PolicyEngine::authorize`.
3. Emit an audit record for allow/deny (redacted summary).
4. If allowed: perform the injection via the WezTerm client.
5. Return a structured outcome suitable for robot/human/workflow logging.

All user-facing send commands and workflow action executors must call this helper. There should be **exactly one** implementation of “send with policy”.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - policy deny prevents calling the underlying WezTerm send API
  - RequireApproval returns a stable allow-once payload (when wired)

- Integration tests:
  - smoke test `wa robot send` against a synthetic policy/capability state

## Acceptance Criteria
- Attempting to inject input while pane state is `AltScreen` is denied everywhere.
- There is only one injection implementation shared by robot/human/workflow paths (no duplicated “send-with-policy” logic).

 {"id":"wa-4vx.8.6","title":"Policy tests: capability gates, rate limiting, redaction, approval behavior","description":"# Task: Policy tests

## Goal
Prevent regressions in safety-critical logic.

Policy regressions are high-impact because they either:
- block safe operations (user-hostile), or
- allow unsafe operations (trust-destroying)

## Testing
Unit tests must cover:
- Capability gates
  - deny SendText in AltScreen
  - deny SendText when recent_gap is true
  - require prompt_active (or RequireApproval if configured)

- Rate limiting
  - per-pane/per-action budgets
  - no runaway loops

- Redaction
  - inputs and secrets never appear in logs/audit summaries

- RequireApproval + allow-once approvals
  - RequireApproval returns a scoped allow-once payload
  - approval enables ONLY the matching action fingerprint
  - TTL expiry works
  - Deny decisions cannot be overridden via approval

- **Reservations**
  - non-owner actions are denied/require-approval per config
  - owner actions are allowed
  - expired reservations are treated as no reservation

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests fail loudly on any policy behavior change.
- Adding a new policy rule requires adding/adjusting tests.


LABELS: area-safety, area-tests, phase-1

DEPENDS ON
  → ○ wa-4vx.8: [EPIC] Safety & policy engine (capability gates, rate limits, redaction) ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.1: Define policy model: ActionKind, PolicyDecision, authorize() API (capabilities provided separately) ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.3: Secret redaction for audit logs (tokens, api keys, passwords) + safe logging conventions ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.8: PaneCapabilities derivation: PromptActive/CommandRunning/AltScreen/RecentGap (deterministic first, safe fallback) ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.9: Approval tokens (allow-once): ergonomic override for RequireApproval decisions ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.2: Rate limiting: per-pane, per-action-kind (avoid spam / runaway loops) ● P1
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.4: Policy rules loaded from config (allow/deny/require approval) + robot-safe errors ● P1
  → ○ wa-nu4.1.6.2: Enforce reservations in PolicyEngine + send paths ● P2

 {"id":"wa-4vx.8.7","title":"Audit trail emission: record allow/deny for every action (send, workflow steps, MCP) with redaction","description":"# Task: Audit trail emission (core)

## Goal
Ensure the earliest, most safety-relevant actions are auditable from day 1:
- `wa robot send` / `wa send` allow/deny/require-approval decisions
- `wa approve` (approval grants)

This provides user trust and postmortem visibility without waiting for workflows/MCP.

## Actions covered (core)
- `wa robot send` / `wa send`
  - allowed actions
  - denied actions
  - require-approval actions (with allow-once metadata)
- `wa approve` (approval grant)
- **reservation conflicts** (deny/require-approval when a pane is reserved by someone else)

## Requirements
- Audit entries must be written even on denial.
- Audit must include:
  - who initiated (actor kind + actor id)
  - policy decision + reason (+ rule id when relevant)
  - preconditions observed (prompt active, alt-screen, gaps)
  - verification outcomes (wait-for matched, timeout)
- Redaction:
  - never store raw secrets or full inputs
  - store hashes/summaries consistent with the redaction system

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - an allow/deny decision produces a correctly shaped audit record
  - redaction removes secret-like substrings from summaries
- Integration tests:
  - `wa robot send` denied → audit row exists
  - require-approval → audit row exists and includes allow-once metadata
  - reservation conflict produces an audit row with reservation metadata

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every attempted action (including denials) creates an audit entry.
- Audit records are safe by default (redacted) and sufficient to explain the decision.


LABELS: area-audit, area-safety, phase-1

DEPENDS ON
  → ○ wa-4vx.3.8: Audit trail storage: audit_actions table + queries + retention/redaction hooks ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8: [EPIC] Safety & policy engine (capability gates, rate limits, redaction) ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.1: Define policy model: ActionKind, PolicyDecision, authorize() API (capabilities provided separately) ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.3: Secret redaction for audit logs (tokens, api keys, passwords) + safe logging conventions ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.5: Integrate PolicyEngine into all SendText paths (robot send + workflows) ● P0
  → ○ wa-4vx.8.9: Approval tokens (allow-once): ergonomic override for RequireApproval decisions ● P0

BLOCKS
  ← ○ wa-4vx.7.4: Implement `wa robot send` (PolicyEngine-gated, optional wait-for, timeout) ● P0
  ← ○ wa-nu4.1.1.11: Workflow audit integration: record per-step actions/outcomes into audit trail ● P1
  ← ○ wa-4vx.10.10: E2E script: policy gating (AltScreen/prompt-required/gap) denies sends with correct audit + errors ● P2
  ← ○ wa-4vx.10.16: E2E script: RequireApproval → wa approve allow-once → send succeeds (audited) ● P2
  ← ○ wa-4vx.10.18: E2E script: secret redaction in audit/export (no raw secrets in artifacts) ● P2
  ← ○ wa-4vx.10.25: E2E script: command safety gate blocks destructive-looking sends (optional dcg) ● P2

  ← ○ wa-nu4.3.2.12: [Human command] wa approve (grant allow-once approval code) ● P2

-{"id":"wa-4vx.8.8","title":"PaneCapabilities derivation: PromptActive/CommandRunning/AltScreen/RecentGap (deterministic first, safe fallback)","description":"# Task: PaneCapabilities derivation (state → capability gates)

## Goal
Define and maintain a **deterministic** `PaneCapabilities` snapshot for each pane that policy/workflows can trust.

This is the missing link between:
- ingest signals (OSC 133 markers, gap detection, optional status_update IPC)
- safety/policy decisions (whether it is safe to send input)

## Capabilities (v0)
- `prompt_active: bool` (preferred: OSC 133 prompt markers)
- `command_running: bool` (OSC 133 start/end)
- `recent_gap: bool` (true if we emitted a GAP since last verified prompt boundary)
- `alt_screen: Option<bool>`
  - `Some(true)` when confidently detected
  - `Some(false)` when confidently not alt-screen
  - `None` when unknown

## Data sources / precedence
- Deterministic first (preferred):
  - OSC 133 markers
  - explicit GAP events
  - **status_update IPC** (if available) for alt-screen + cursor/dimensions
- Fallback heuristics (careful):
  - only used when deterministic signals are unavailable

## Safety behavior
- If `alt_screen` is unknown, policy should default to deny or require approval for `SendText`.

## Testing
- Unit tests cover state transitions:
  - prompt active ↔ command running
  - gap causes `recent_gap=true`
  - `recent_gap` clears after verified prompt boundary
  - alt-screen transitions when status_update is available

## Acceptance Criteria
- Unit tests cover capability state transitions and gap clearing semantics.
- Policy consumers can rely on these fields being stable and deterministic.

  - `Some(false)` when confidently not alt-screen
  - `None` when unknown

## Data sources / precedence
- Deterministic first (preferred):
  - OSC 133 markers
  - explicit GAP events
  - **status_update IPC** (if available) for alt-screen + cursor/dimensions
- Fallback heuristics (careful):
  - only used when deterministic signals are unavailable

## Safety behavior
- If `alt_screen` is unknown, policy should default to deny or require approval for `SendText`.

## Testing
- Unit tests cover state transitions:
  - prompt active ↔ command running
  - gap causes `recent_gap=true`
  - `recent_gap` clears after verified prompt boundary
  - alt-screen transitions when status_update is available

## Acceptance Criteria
- Unit tests cover capability state transitions and gap clearing semantics.
- Policy consumers can rely on these fields being stable and deterministic.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.8.9","title":"Approval tokens (allow-once): ergonomic override for RequireApproval decisions","description":"# Task: Approval tokens (allow-once)

## Goal
When PolicyEngine returns RequireApproval, provide an ergonomic, safe way for a human to explicitly approve the action without editing config files.

This is essential UX:
- robots/agents can surface a structured error with an "approval code"
- a human can review and grant the approval intentionally
- wa can proceed safely with a minimal blast radius (scoped + expiring)

## Non-goals
- This must NOT override hard Deny decisions.
- This must NOT store secrets in plaintext.

## Design (v0)
### Approval request generation
- On RequireApproval, return a structured payload containing:
  - allow_once_code (short)
  - allow_once_full_hash (long, e.g., sha256:...)
  - expires_at (TTL)
  - human-readable approval summary
  - canonical command the user can run (e.g., `wa approve <code>`)

### Scoping
Approval must be scoped tightly to prevent confused-deputy bugs:
- workspace scope
- action kind (SendText / WorkflowStep / MCP tool)
- pane_id (when applicable)
- action fingerprint (normalized)

### Storage
- Persist approvals in DB (workspace-scoped) with TTL.

### Audit
- Approval grants are audited.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - fingerprint determinism
  - TTL expiry works
  - wrong workspace/pane/action_kind does not match
  - RequireApproval never becomes Allow without explicit approval

## Acceptance Criteria
- A RequireApproval result includes a stable allow-once payload suitable for robot and human surfaces.
- Approvals are scoped, expiring, and auditable.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.9","title":"[EPIC] Unified configuration (wa.toml + env overrides + validation)","description":"# Unified configuration

## Goal
Implement a single configuration system that controls wa behavior consistently across CLI/daemon/robot/mcp.

## Why this matters
Without a unified config, behavior becomes inconsistent and brittle. Config must be:
- validated (schema + semantic checks)
- overrideable (env vars + CLI flags)
- reloadable (for safe settings)

## Deliverables
- `~/.config/wa/wa.toml` (and data dir under `~/.local/share/wa` by default)
- config loader:
  - merges default + file + env + CLI
  - emits an "effective config" output
- `wa config` commands:
  - init, validate, show --effective, set
- hot reload for non-destructive settings (poll intervals, retention, log level)

## Acceptance
- `wa config validate` catches invalid configs.
- `wa watch --reload-config` updates poll intervals without restart.



## Success Criteria
- A single config loader produces an effective config used consistently across `wa watch`, robot, human CLI, and MCP.
- Workspace resolution yields correct default paths (DB/log/lock/socket) with clear precedence (flags > env > config > defaults).
- Config validation catches invalid settings with actionable errors.
- Pane include/exclude rules are configurable and enforced in ingest to protect privacy and reduce load.
- Tests cover parse/serialize, overrides, validation, and workspace path resolution.


## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Parse/serialize roundtrip for `wa.toml` (including unknown fields behavior if we allow it).
  - Env + CLI overrides precedence and error messages.
  - Workspace path resolution across OS conventions (XDG, macOS) with deterministic temp dirs.

- Integration tests:
  - “effective config” snapshot tests for representative configs.
  - Hot reload test: mutate config on disk, ensure watcher picks up allowed changes and rejects unsafe ones.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Deliverables.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.9.1","title":"Define wa.toml schema (ingest/storage/patterns/workflows/safety/metrics) + defaults","description":"# Task: Define `wa.toml` schema + defaults

## Goal
Define the unified configuration object (Rust structs) and defaults so wa behavior is consistent across:
- `wa watch` (daemon)
- `wa robot ...`
- human CLI
- MCP server

## Required sections (v0)
- `general`:
  - log level
  - workspace/data dir
- `ingest`:
  - poll interval
  - concurrency/backpressure limits
  - gap detection thresholds
- `storage`:
  - DB path (workspace-derived)
  - retention settings
  - checkpoint/flush intervals
- `patterns`:
  - enabled packs
  - per-pack config overrides
- `workflows` (Phase 2 consumes):
  - enable/disable workflows
  - allowlist/denylist for auto-run
  - max concurrent workflows
- `safety`:
  - capability gating rules
  - rate limits
  - approval (allow-once) parameters
  - redaction settings
  - **reservation defaults** (default TTL, max TTL, conflict behavior)
- `metrics`:
  - enable + bind

## Deliverables
- Rust config structs with `serde` for TOML.
- Explicit default values aligned with the plan (sensible for local development, safe by default).
- Forward-compatibility posture:
  - allow unknown fields where possible (so adding new config doesn’t brick older builds)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - defaults serialize to valid TOML
  - defaults round-trip parse
  - missing optional sections do not break parsing

## Acceptance Criteria
- Default config can be serialized to TOML and parsed back successfully.
- The schema surface is sufficient to configure ingest/storage/patterns/workflows/safety without adding ad-hoc flags.

+// ============================================================================
+// Policy Gated Injector (wa-4vx.8.5)

+
+/// Result of a policy-gated injection attempt
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
+#[serde(tag = "status", rename_all = "snake_case")]
+pub enum InjectionResult {
+    /// Injection was allowed and executed
+    Allowed {
+        /// The policy decision (for audit)
+        decision: PolicyDecision,
+        /// Redacted summary of what was sent
+        summary: String,
+        /// Pane ID that received the injection
+        pane_id: u64,
+        /// Action kind that was performed
+        action: ActionKind,
+    },
+    /// Injection was denied by policy
+    Denied {
+        /// The policy decision with reason
+        decision: PolicyDecision,
+        /// Redacted summary of what was attempted
+        summary: String,
+        /// Pane ID that was targeted
+        pane_id: u64,
+        /// Action kind that was attempted
+        action: ActionKind,
+    },
+    /// Injection requires approval before proceeding
+    RequiresApproval {
+        /// The policy decision with approval details
+        decision: PolicyDecision,
+        /// Redacted summary of what was attempted
+        summary: String,
+        /// Pane ID that was targeted
+        pane_id: u64,
+        /// Action kind that was attempted
+        action: ActionKind,
+    },
+    /// Injection failed due to an error (after policy allowed)
+    Error {
+        /// Error message
+        error: String,
+        /// Pane ID that was targeted
+        pane_id: u64,
+        /// Action kind that was attempted
+        action: ActionKind,
+    },
+}
+
+impl InjectionResult {
+    /// Check if the injection succeeded
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn is_allowed(&self) -> bool {
+        matches!(self, Self::Allowed { .. })
+    }
+
+    /// Check if the injection was denied
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn is_denied(&self) -> bool {
+        matches!(self, Self::Denied { .. })
+    }
+
+    /// Check if the injection requires approval
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn requires_approval(&self) -> bool {
+        matches!(self, Self::RequiresApproval { .. })
+    }
+
+    /// Get the error message if this is an error result
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn error_message(&self) -> Option<&str> {
+        match self {
+            Self::Error { error, .. } => Some(error),
+            _ => None,
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Get the rule ID that caused denial or approval requirement
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn rule_id(&self) -> Option<&str> {
+        match self {
+            Self::Denied { decision, .. } | Self::RequiresApproval { decision, .. } => {
+                decision.rule_id()
+            }
+            _ => None,
+        }
+    }
+}
+
+/// Policy-gated input injector
+///
+/// This is the **single implementation** of "send with policy" that all
+/// user-facing send commands and workflow action executors must use.
+///
+/// # Responsibilities
+///
+/// 1. Build a `PolicyInput` (actor kind, pane id, action kind, redacted summary)
+/// 2. Call `PolicyEngine::authorize`
+/// 3. If allowed: perform the injection via the WezTerm client
+/// 4. Return a structured outcome suitable for robot/human/workflow logging
+///
+/// # Safety
+///
+/// Attempting to inject input while pane state is `AltScreen` will be denied.
+/// Unknown alt-screen state also triggers denial (conservative by default).
+///
+/// # Example
+///
+/// ```ignore
+/// use wa_core::policy::{PolicyGatedInjector, PolicyEngine, ActorKind};
+/// use wa_core::wezterm::WeztermClient;
+///
+/// let engine = PolicyEngine::default();
+/// let client = WeztermClient::new();
+/// let injector = PolicyGatedInjector::new(engine, client);
+///
+/// // Capabilities are derived from current pane state
+/// let caps = PaneCapabilities::prompt();
+/// let result = injector.send_text(1, "ls -la", ActorKind::Robot, &caps).await;
+///
+/// match result {
+///     InjectionResult::Allowed { .. } => println!("Sent successfully"),
+///     InjectionResult::Denied { decision, .. } => println!("Denied: {:?}", decision),
+///     InjectionResult::RequiresApproval { decision, .. } => println!("Needs approval"),
+///     InjectionResult::Error { error, .. } => println!("Error: {}", error),
+/// }
+/// ```
+pub struct PolicyGatedInjector {
+    engine: PolicyEngine,
+    client: crate::wezterm::WeztermClient,
+}
+
+impl PolicyGatedInjector {
+    /// Create a new policy-gated injector
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn new(engine: PolicyEngine, client: crate::wezterm::WeztermClient) -> Self {
+        Self { engine, client }
+    }
+
+    /// Create with a permissive policy engine (for testing)
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn permissive(client: crate::wezterm::WeztermClient) -> Self {
+        Self::new(PolicyEngine::permissive(), client)
+    }
+
+    /// Get mutable access to the policy engine
+    pub fn engine_mut(&mut self) -> &mut PolicyEngine {
+        &mut self.engine
+    }
+
+    /// Get the policy engine reference
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn engine(&self) -> &PolicyEngine {
+        &self.engine
+    }
+
+    /// Send text to a pane with policy gating
+    ///
+    /// This is the primary method for sending text. It:
+    /// 1. Builds policy input with the given capabilities
+    /// 2. Checks command safety gate (dangerous command detection)
+    /// 3. Authorizes via PolicyEngine
+    /// 4. If allowed, sends via WeztermClient
+    /// 5. Returns structured result for audit
+    pub async fn send_text(
+        &mut self,
+        pane_id: u64,
+        text: &str,
+        actor: ActorKind,
+        capabilities: &PaneCapabilities,
+        workflow_id: Option<&str>,
+    ) -> InjectionResult {
+        self.inject(
+            pane_id,
+            text,
+            ActionKind::SendText,
+            actor,
+            capabilities,
+            workflow_id,
+        )
+        .await
+    }
+
+    /// Send Ctrl-C (interrupt) to a pane with policy gating
+    pub async fn send_ctrl_c(
+        &mut self,
+        pane_id: u64,
+        actor: ActorKind,
+        capabilities: &PaneCapabilities,
+        workflow_id: Option<&str>,
+    ) -> InjectionResult {
+        self.inject(
+            pane_id,
+            crate::wezterm::control::CTRL_C,
+            ActionKind::SendCtrlC,
+            actor,
+            capabilities,
+            workflow_id,
+        )
+        .await
+    }
+
+    /// Send Ctrl-D (EOF) to a pane with policy gating
+    pub async fn send_ctrl_d(
+        &mut self,
+        pane_id: u64,
+        actor: ActorKind,
+        capabilities: &PaneCapabilities,
+        workflow_id: Option<&str>,
+    ) -> InjectionResult {
+        self.inject(
+            pane_id,
+            crate::wezterm::control::CTRL_D,
+            ActionKind::SendCtrlD,
+            actor,
+            capabilities,
+            workflow_id,
+        )
+        .await
+    }
+
+    /// Send Ctrl-Z (suspend) to a pane with policy gating
+    pub async fn send_ctrl_z(
+        &mut self,
+        pane_id: u64,
+        actor: ActorKind,
+        capabilities: &PaneCapabilities,
+        workflow_id: Option<&str>,
+    ) -> InjectionResult {
+        self.inject(
+            pane_id,
+            crate::wezterm::control::CTRL_Z,
+            ActionKind::SendCtrlZ,
+            actor,
+            capabilities,
+            workflow_id,
+        )
+        .await
+    }
+
+    /// Send any control character to a pane with policy gating
+    pub async fn send_control(
+        &mut self,
+        pane_id: u64,
+        control_char: &str,
+        actor: ActorKind,
+        capabilities: &PaneCapabilities,
+        workflow_id: Option<&str>,
+    ) -> InjectionResult {
+        self.inject(
+            pane_id,
+            control_char,
+            ActionKind::SendControl,
+            actor,
+            capabilities,
+            workflow_id,
+        )
+        .await
+    }
+
+    /// Internal injection method with policy gating
+    async fn inject(
+        &mut self,
+        pane_id: u64,
+        text: &str,
+        action: ActionKind,
+        actor: ActorKind,
+        capabilities: &PaneCapabilities,
+        workflow_id: Option<&str>,
+    ) -> InjectionResult {
+        // Create redacted summary for audit
+        let summary = self.engine.redact_secrets(text);
+
+        // Build policy input
+        let mut input = PolicyInput::new(action, actor)
+            .with_pane(pane_id)
+            .with_capabilities(capabilities.clone())
+            .with_text_summary(&summary);
+
+        // Add workflow context if present
+        if let Some(wf_id) = workflow_id {
+            input = input.with_workflow(wf_id);
+        }
+
+        // For SendText, add command text for safety gate
+        if action == ActionKind::SendText {
+            input = input.with_command_text(text);
+        }
+
+        // Authorize
+        let decision = self.engine.authorize(&input);
+
+        match &decision {
+            PolicyDecision::Allow => {
+                // SAFETY: This is the only place where actual injection happens
+                // after policy approval. The text reference lifetime is handled
+                // by copying to a String for the actual send.
+                let text_owned = text.to_string();
+                let client = &self.client;
+
+                // We need to call the send function with owned data
+                let result = match action {
+                    ActionKind::SendText => client.send_text(pane_id, &text_owned).await,
+                    ActionKind::SendCtrlC => client.send_ctrl_c(pane_id).await,
+                    ActionKind::SendCtrlD => client.send_ctrl_d(pane_id).await,
+                    ActionKind::SendCtrlZ => client.send_control(pane_id, crate::wezterm::control::CTRL_Z).await,
+                    ActionKind::SendControl => client.send_control(pane_id, &text_owned).await,
+                    _ => unreachable!("inject called with non-injection action"),
+                };
+
+                match result {
+                    Ok(()) => InjectionResult::Allowed {
+                        decision,
+                        summary,
+                        pane_id,
+                        action,
+                    },
+                    Err(e) => InjectionResult::Error {
+                        error: e.to_string(),
+                        pane_id,
+                        action,
+                    },
+                }
+            }
+            PolicyDecision::Deny { .. } => InjectionResult::Denied {
+                decision,
+                summary,
+                pane_id,
+                action,
+            },
+            PolicyDecision::RequireApproval { .. } => InjectionResult::RequiresApproval {
+                decision,
+                summary,
+                pane_id,
+                action,
+            },
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Redact text using the policy engine's redactor
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn redact(&self, text: &str) -> String {
+        self.engine.redact_secrets(text)
+    }
+}
+
